texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A small-scale stand-alone solar-driven membrane desalination unit was designed and manufactured under project funding agreement number ICA3-CT-2002-10025 (SMADES). The desalination plant is intended for autonomous operation in arid and semi-arid remote regions with a lack of electricity and drinkable water but with high solar irradiation. The plant integrates solar thermal and PV energy. The desalination energy is supplied entirely by flatplate solar thermal collectors and the electrical auxiliary energy is supplied by a PV panel. Feed water is directly heated in the absorber of the collector. The desalination system is composed of a spiral-wound air-gap membrane distillation module with internal heat recovery function. The main advantages of the plant are: no need for external electricity, no additional water for cooling, and no impact on the environment. The plant was installed at the Jordan University of Science and Technology (JUST) campus and prepared for long-term testing in the weather conditions of Irbid, Jordan. The plant has been continuously operated producing as high as 120 L/d with an approximate distillate conductivity of 5 µS/cm. The thermal energy required by the process was in the range of 200-300 kWh/m 3 .
texts.text: A small-scale stand-alone solar-driven membrane desalination unit was designed and manufactured under project funding agreement number ICA3-CT-2002-10025 (SMADES). The desalination plant is intended for autonomous operation in arid and semi-arid remote regions with a lack of electricity and drinkable water but with high solar irradiation. The plant integrates solar thermal and PV energy. The desalination energy is supplied entirely by flatplate solar thermal collectors and the electrical auxiliary energy is supplied by a PV panel. Feed water is directly heated in the absorber of the collector. The desalination system is composed of a spiral-wound air-gap membrane distillation module with internal heat recovery function. The main advantages of the plant are: no need for external electricity, no additional water for cooling, and no impact on the environment. The plant was installed at the Jordan University of Science and Technology (JUST) campus and prepared for long-term testing in the weather conditions of Irbid, Jordan. The plant has been continuously operated producing as high as 120 L/d with an approximate distillate conductivity of 5 µS/cm. The thermal energy required by the process was in the range of 200-300 kWh/m 3 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The integration of solar heat into desalination processes is important to both the environment and the production cost. Solar thermal systems, using  the  sun  as  fuel  has  low  environmental impact. In applying solar heat, attention must be paid to the time dependency of the solar energy supply and the heat demand of the desalination processes. Favorable conditions for solar thermal energy mean temperatures which are as low as possible.
texts.text: The integration of solar heat into desalination processes is important to both the environment and the production cost. Solar thermal systems, using  the  sun  as  fuel  has  low  environmental impact. In applying solar heat, attention must be paid to the time dependency of the solar energy supply and the heat demand of the desalination processes. Favorable conditions for solar thermal energy mean temperatures which are as low as possible.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Solar energy can be used for seawater desalination  either  by  producing  the  thermal  energy required to drive the phase-change processes or by producing the electricity required to drive the membrane processes. Solar desalination processes can  be  devised  in  two  main  types:  direct  and indirect collection systems. The 'direct method' uses solar energy to produce distillate directly in the solar collector, whereas in indirect collection systems, two sub-systems are employed (one for solar  energy  collection,  the  other  for  desalination). The direct solar energy method uses a variety  of  simple  stills  suitable  for  very  small water demands; indirect methods use thermal or electrical energy and can be classified as: distillation  methods  using  solar  collectors  or membrane methods using solar collectors and/or photovoltaics for power generation.
texts.text: Solar energy can be used for seawater desalination  either  by  producing  the  thermal  energy required to drive the phase-change processes or by producing the electricity required to drive the membrane processes. Solar desalination processes can  be  devised  in  two  main  types:  direct  and indirect collection systems. The 'direct method' uses solar energy to produce distillate directly in the solar collector, whereas in indirect collection systems, two sub-systems are employed (one for solar  energy  collection,  the  other  for  desalination). The direct solar energy method uses a variety  of  simple  stills  suitable  for  very  small water demands; indirect methods use thermal or electrical energy and can be classified as: distillation  methods  using  solar  collectors  or membrane methods using solar collectors and/or photovoltaics for power generation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Solar  thermal  desalination  plants  utilizing indirect collection of solar energy can be classified  into  the  following  categories:  atmospheric humidification/dehumidification, multi-stage flash  (MSF),  multi-effect  distillation  (MED), vapor  compression  (VC)  and  membrane  distillation (MD).
texts.text: Solar  thermal  desalination  plants  utilizing indirect collection of solar energy can be classified  into  the  following  categories:  atmospheric humidification/dehumidification, multi-stage flash  (MSF),  multi-effect  distillation  (MED), vapor  compression  (VC)  and  membrane  distillation (MD).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation is a hybrid of thermal distillation  and  membrane  processes  best  described as transmembrane evaporation.  The driving force is a temperature difference between the feed water and permeate which results in a vapor pressure differential across a hydrophobic porous  membrane  (in  contrast  to  pressure  as driving force for RO and electrical potential as driving force for ED). Vapor evolved from the feed  solution  passes  through  the  pores  of  the membrane  and  is  collected  on  the  other  side. Since liquid does not penetrate the hydrophobic membrane, dissolved ions are completely rejected by the membrane.
texts.text: Membrane distillation is a hybrid of thermal distillation  and  membrane  processes  best  described as transmembrane evaporation.  The driving force is a temperature difference between the feed water and permeate which results in a vapor pressure differential across a hydrophobic porous  membrane  (in  contrast  to  pressure  as driving force for RO and electrical potential as driving force for ED). Vapor evolved from the feed  solution  passes  through  the  pores  of  the membrane  and  is  collected  on  the  other  side. Since liquid does not penetrate the hydrophobic membrane, dissolved ions are completely rejected by the membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Methods  for  collecting  the  vapor  permeate include immediate condensation within a colder liquid flowing on the second side of the membrane or condensation on a cold surface located at some distance from the membrane. In the latter situation, vacuum can be applied to draw more vapors through the membrane. The materials used most commonly to produce hydrophobic membranes  suitable  for  membrane  distillation  are polypropylene (PP), polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), and polyvinyldeneflouride (PVDF). Nonetheless,  the  number  of  commercial  applications  of  membrane  distillation  is  small  but growing.
texts.text: Methods  for  collecting  the  vapor  permeate include immediate condensation within a colder liquid flowing on the second side of the membrane or condensation on a cold surface located at some distance from the membrane. In the latter situation, vacuum can be applied to draw more vapors through the membrane. The materials used most commonly to produce hydrophobic membranes  suitable  for  membrane  distillation  are polypropylene (PP), polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), and polyvinyldeneflouride (PVDF). Nonetheless,  the  number  of  commercial  applications  of  membrane  distillation  is  small  but growing.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Few demonstration projects using solarthermal  membrane distillation  have  been  built. Hogan et al. [1], at the University of New South Wales in Australia, describe a 0.05 m 3 /d system using  3  m 2 of  solar  collectors.  Their  system, which  was  tested  in  Sydney,  consisted  of  a hollow-fiber  membrane  module  for  MD  and  a heat  recovery  exchanger  for  reducing  capital costs. This solar-powered membrane distillation (SPMD) unit was found to be technically feasible, with  the  membrane  process  being  compatible with  the  transient  nature  of  the  energy  source. The calculated  efficiency  of  17  L/d/m 2 of  collector area compares favorably with solar MSF and ME plants. The researchers calculated that the process requires 55.6 kWh/m 3 (thermal and electric).
texts.text: Few demonstration projects using solarthermal  membrane distillation  have  been  built. Hogan et al. [1], at the University of New South Wales in Australia, describe a 0.05 m 3 /d system using  3  m 2 of  solar  collectors.  Their  system, which  was  tested  in  Sydney,  consisted  of  a hollow-fiber  membrane  module  for  MD  and  a heat  recovery  exchanger  for  reducing  capital costs. This solar-powered membrane distillation (SPMD) unit was found to be technically feasible, with  the  membrane  process  being  compatible with  the  transient  nature  of  the  energy  source. The calculated  efficiency  of  17  L/d/m 2 of  collector area compares favorably with solar MSF and ME plants. The researchers calculated that the process requires 55.6 kWh/m 3 (thermal and electric).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Bier  and  Plantikow  [2]  constructed  another SPMD using an  air  gap  membrane  distillation (AGMD) module  instead  of  the  direct  contact membrane distillation (DCMD) module tested in the SPMD pilot plant constructed by Hogan et al.
texts.text: Bier  and  Plantikow  [2]  constructed  another SPMD using an  air  gap  membrane  distillation (AGMD) module  instead  of  the  direct  contact membrane distillation (DCMD) module tested in the SPMD pilot plant constructed by Hogan et al.

texts.text: F. Banat et al. / Desalination 217 (2007) 29-37

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The latent heat recovery process in this late pilot plant was integrated with MD in a spiral-wound membrane module. However, the additional mass transfer resistance created by the air gap resulted in a large reduction in the trans-membrane water flux.  Recently,  Koschikowski  et  al.  [3]  used  a similar membrane module to that used by Bier et al. According to their calculations, without heat storage, the plant can distill 150 L/d of water in the summer in a southern country.
texts.text: The latent heat recovery process in this late pilot plant was integrated with MD in a spiral-wound membrane module. However, the additional mass transfer resistance created by the air gap resulted in a large reduction in the trans-membrane water flux.  Recently,  Koschikowski  et  al.  [3]  used  a similar membrane module to that used by Bier et al. According to their calculations, without heat storage, the plant can distill 150 L/d of water in the summer in a southern country.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Banat et al. [4] integrated a MD module with a  solar  still  to  produce  potable  water  from simulated  seawater.  In  their  investigations  the solar still was used for both seawater heating and potable  water  production.  The  effect  of  some factors affecting flux of the membrane module was also investigated. Their experimental studies showed that the contribution of the solar still in the distillate production was no more than 20% of the total flux.
texts.text: Banat et al. [4] integrated a MD module with a  solar  still  to  produce  potable  water  from simulated  seawater.  In  their  investigations  the solar still was used for both seawater heating and potable  water  production.  The  effect  of  some factors affecting flux of the membrane module was also investigated. Their experimental studies showed that the contribution of the solar still in the distillate production was no more than 20% of the total flux.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The desalination performance and O&M procedures of an air-gap type membrane distillation system was tested by Walton et al. [5] using lowgrade  thermal  energy  (between  13  and  75ºC) supplied by a salt-gradient solar pond. The entire system (an air gap membrane distillation module, 2.94 m 2 plus controlling pumps and heaters) built by Scarab (Sweden) was used in this study. Hot brine was pumped from the bottom of the solar pond  and  passed  through  a  heat  exchanger  to supply  heat.  Cold  water  from  the  solar  pond surface was passed through a heat exchanger to provide cooling. High and low temperatures for system operation were obtained by changing the flow rates for solar pond hot and cold water. The permeate  flux  was  fluctuating  and  reached  a maximum of 6 L/m 2 .h.
texts.text: The desalination performance and O&M procedures of an air-gap type membrane distillation system was tested by Walton et al. [5] using lowgrade  thermal  energy  (between  13  and  75ºC) supplied by a salt-gradient solar pond. The entire system (an air gap membrane distillation module, 2.94 m 2 plus controlling pumps and heaters) built by Scarab (Sweden) was used in this study. Hot brine was pumped from the bottom of the solar pond  and  passed  through  a  heat  exchanger  to supply  heat.  Cold  water  from  the  solar  pond surface was passed through a heat exchanger to provide cooling. High and low temperatures for system operation were obtained by changing the flow rates for solar pond hot and cold water. The permeate  flux  was  fluctuating  and  reached  a maximum of 6 L/m 2 .h.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theoretical calculations, based upon measured results, indicate that membrane distillation with latent heat recovery can be easily implemented and that this modification would make MD competitive with other thermal technologies in terms of energy use. The researchers found that membrane distillation is only competitive relative to reverse  osmosis  when  low  cost  heat  energy  is available and/or when the water chemistry of the source water is too difficult for treatment with reverse osmosis.
texts.text: Theoretical calculations, based upon measured results, indicate that membrane distillation with latent heat recovery can be easily implemented and that this modification would make MD competitive with other thermal technologies in terms of energy use. The researchers found that membrane distillation is only competitive relative to reverse  osmosis  when  low  cost  heat  energy  is available and/or when the water chemistry of the source water is too difficult for treatment with reverse osmosis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With the aid of a computer simulation program Ding et al. [6] concluded that for a certain solar collector area, heat recovery from the permeate to the feed is the only way to improve energy efficiency of the system, and in this way plant capacity can be promoted.
texts.text: With the aid of a computer simulation program Ding et al. [6] concluded that for a certain solar collector area, heat recovery from the permeate to the feed is the only way to improve energy efficiency of the system, and in this way plant capacity can be promoted.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  the  activities  of  the  SMADES  project financed by the European Commission, first a socalled 'compact SMADES' system was designed and  tested  to  generate  process  parameters  and design  data  needed  for  designing  the  so-called 'large SMADES' system. The overall objective of the SMADES project was the development of stand-alone desalination systems with very low maintenance needs for arid and semi-arid remote regions with a lack of drinkable water but have brackish  or  seawater  along  with  a  high  solar irradiation. The compact system was installed at the campus of the Jordan University of Science and Technology, Irbid, Jordan. A full description of  the  compact  system  and  some  of  the  main results obtained are presented in this paper.
texts.text: In  the  activities  of  the  SMADES  project financed by the European Commission, first a socalled 'compact SMADES' system was designed and  tested  to  generate  process  parameters  and design  data  needed  for  designing  the  so-called 'large SMADES' system. The overall objective of the SMADES project was the development of stand-alone desalination systems with very low maintenance needs for arid and semi-arid remote regions with a lack of drinkable water but have brackish  or  seawater  along  with  a  high  solar irradiation. The compact system was installed at the campus of the Jordan University of Science and Technology, Irbid, Jordan. A full description of  the  compact  system  and  some  of  the  main results obtained are presented in this paper.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A flow diagram of the solar-powered membrane distillation (SPMD) process is shown in Fig.1. The system has four major components: a MD module, solar energy collector, feed tank and a PV module.
texts.text: A flow diagram of the solar-powered membrane distillation (SPMD) process is shown in Fig.1. The system has four major components: a MD module, solar energy collector, feed tank and a PV module.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the MD module, the hot water is directed along  the  membrane,  passing  the  evaporator channel from its inlet to its outlet while cooling down. The feed water is pumped from the feed tank (475 L) and passes through the condenser channel  in  a  counter  flow  from  its  inlet  to  its outlet  while  warming  up  .The  partial  pressure difference caused by the temperature difference on  both  sides  of  the  membrane  is  the  driving

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  these  experiments  PTFE  membrane  was used with 0.2 µm pore diameters, 35 µm thickness and 80% porosity. The flow channels were made by spiral winding of membrane and condenser foils to form a spiral-wound module with an effective membrane area of 10 m². Fraunhofer ISE (Germany) developed and manufactured this membrane module.
texts.text: In  these  experiments  PTFE  membrane  was used with 0.2 µm pore diameters, 35 µm thickness and 80% porosity. The flow channels were made by spiral winding of membrane and condenser foils to form a spiral-wound module with an effective membrane area of 10 m². Fraunhofer ISE (Germany) developed and manufactured this membrane module.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  feed stream is reheated by  passing through a 5.73-m² corrosion-free solar collector (Fentek, Turkey) and directed to the membrane as a hot stream. When the stream comes out of the membrane module it goes back to the feed tank. The  solar collector glass used is  produced specially for solar collectors, this type of glass contains low amount of iron and has a tempered structure  and  a  special  texture.  Because  of  its structure, its light transmission is as high as 92 % in contrast to the other glasses. Fig. 2 is a photograph of the compact system.
texts.text: The  feed stream is reheated by  passing through a 5.73-m² corrosion-free solar collector (Fentek, Turkey) and directed to the membrane as a hot stream. When the stream comes out of the membrane module it goes back to the feed tank. The  solar collector glass used is  produced specially for solar collectors, this type of glass contains low amount of iron and has a tempered structure  and  a  special  texture.  Because  of  its structure, its light transmission is as high as 92 % in contrast to the other glasses. Fig. 2 is a photograph of the compact system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since photovoltaics convert solar  energy directly into electrical energy, this technology is best suited for coupling with DC pumps. A PV module of 1000 W/m² was used to supply electricity for the feed pump and magnetic valves; the total area of the module is (1310×654) mm².
texts.text: Since photovoltaics convert solar  energy directly into electrical energy, this technology is best suited for coupling with DC pumps. A PV module of 1000 W/m² was used to supply electricity for the feed pump and magnetic valves; the total area of the module is (1310×654) mm².

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A series of temperature sensors (B&B, Germany) were installed on the feed and return lines to the membrane. Two flow meters were installed to measure the volumetric flow rate of distillate (Bio-Technology, USA) and feed (Krohne, Germany). Conductivity probes (WTW, Germany) were installed in the feed line and in the distillate line in order to measure the source water to be tested and the quality of the distillate. A pyranometer  (Kipp  &  Zonen,  Germany)  was  used  to measure the global irradiation during the operation time. All mentioned sensors and probes were connected to a signal acquisition board (Agilent Technologies,  Germany).  By  using  the  latest version of the software Benchlink Data logger II, data for temperature, global irradiation, flow, and conductivity  were  registered  for  later  analysis. Fig.  3  shows  the  hydraulic  board  with  pipes, pumps, volume flow meters as well as the MD module (in the right bottom corner).
texts.text: A series of temperature sensors (B&B, Germany) were installed on the feed and return lines to the membrane. Two flow meters were installed to measure the volumetric flow rate of distillate (Bio-Technology, USA) and feed (Krohne, Germany). Conductivity probes (WTW, Germany) were installed in the feed line and in the distillate line in order to measure the source water to be tested and the quality of the distillate. A pyranometer  (Kipp  &  Zonen,  Germany)  was  used  to measure the global irradiation during the operation time. All mentioned sensors and probes were connected to a signal acquisition board (Agilent Technologies,  Germany).  By  using  the  latest version of the software Benchlink Data logger II, data for temperature, global irradiation, flow, and conductivity  were  registered  for  later  analysis. Fig.  3  shows  the  hydraulic  board  with  pipes, pumps, volume flow meters as well as the MD module (in the right bottom corner).

texts.orig: The performance of the system for one day in August  2005  is  shown  in  Fig.  4.  The  system started  operation  at  9:00  am  when  the  solar
texts.text: The performance of the system for one day in August  2005  is  shown  in  Fig.  4.  The  system started  operation  at  9:00  am  when  the  solar

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig.  4.  Daily  variations  of  distillate  flow  rate  and conductivity with solar irradiation.
texts.text: Fig.  4.  Daily  variations  of  distillate  flow  rate  and conductivity with solar irradiation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: irradiation was in the range of 600 W/m 2 .  The feed  pump  flow  rate  depends  on  the  current delivered by the PV and increases with increasing the global irradiation. The figure shows how the flow  rate  and  the  conductivity  of  the  distillate varies diurnally. The distillate flow rate followed the behavior of irradiation intensity and reached its  peak value midday (~2.5 L per hour per m 2 membrane  surface  area).  The  conductivity  of distillate was 40 µS/cm in the first few minutes of operation and dropped later to its normal level of 5 µS/cm. Similar behavior was also noticed by Banat and Simandl [7].
texts.text: irradiation was in the range of 600 W/m 2 .  The feed  pump  flow  rate  depends  on  the  current delivered by the PV and increases with increasing the global irradiation. The figure shows how the flow  rate  and  the  conductivity  of  the  distillate varies diurnally. The distillate flow rate followed the behavior of irradiation intensity and reached its  peak value midday (~2.5 L per hour per m 2 membrane  surface  area).  The  conductivity  of distillate was 40 µS/cm in the first few minutes of operation and dropped later to its normal level of 5 µS/cm. Similar behavior was also noticed by Banat and Simandl [7].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  distillate  production  rate  per  day  was normalized  using  the  effective  solar  collector surface area and illustrated in Fig. 5 as a function of  the  sum  of  daily  irradiation.  The  daily  production reached about 19 L/m 2 ap. area when the sum of  daily  irradiation  was  about  7  kWh/m 2 . This value is about 4 times the daily production rate of normal solar stills.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Performance parameters
texts.text: 3.2. Performance parameters

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The lower the value of the specific thermal desalination energy consumption (kWh/m 3 )  the more  economical  is  the  process.  The  module tested was  designed  originally to internally recover the latent heat of vaporization. For this purpose,  the  feed  water  was  directly  used  as coolant for the condenser channel. The condenser and  the  distillate  channel  were  separated  by  a hydrophobic membrane permeable to water in its vapor phase. The  coolant water passes the condenser channel in counter flow to the hot feed water and is gradually warmed by the latent heat of condensation as illustrated in Fig. 7. When the coolant water comes out of the condenser it is fed to the flat plate solar collector for further heating.
texts.text: The lower the value of the specific thermal desalination energy consumption (kWh/m 3 )  the more  economical  is  the  process.  The  module tested was  designed  originally to internally recover the latent heat of vaporization. For this purpose,  the  feed  water  was  directly  used  as coolant for the condenser channel. The condenser and  the  distillate  channel  were  separated  by  a hydrophobic membrane permeable to water in its vapor phase. The  coolant water passes the condenser channel in counter flow to the hot feed water and is gradually warmed by the latent heat of condensation as illustrated in Fig. 7. When the coolant water comes out of the condenser it is fed to the flat plate solar collector for further heating.

texts.orig: The two most commonly encountered performance metrics for solar desalination systems are the gained output ratio (GOR) of the desalination module and thermal efficiency the solar collector. The GOR can either be defined as a mass ratio

texts.orig: The TRR of desalination plants is a measure of its efficiency for producing water. The TRR was in the range of 1-2 (Fig. 10). Thanks to the internal heat recovery in the membrane module the  thermal  recovery  ratio  of  the  system  was always greater than 1.
texts.text: The TRR of desalination plants is a measure of its efficiency for producing water. The TRR was in the range of 1-2 (Fig. 10). Thanks to the internal heat recovery in the membrane module the  thermal  recovery  ratio  of  the  system  was always greater than 1.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Specific thermal energy consumption
texts.text: 3.3. Specific thermal energy consumption

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of the most important characteristics of a thermal seawater-desalination plant is how much heat  it  uses  to  produce  a  specific  quantity  of distillate. The energy used for desalination in the existing system is totally supplied by the solar thermal collectors, the energy for auxiliary equipment as pumps and magnetic valves is supplied by  the  PV  panel.  The  specific  thermal  energy consumption  for  this  system  is  defined  as  the ratio of energy supplied by the solar collector to the  volume  of  distillate  produced.  The  heat recovery function is important to achieve a low specific  energy  consumption  of  the  membrane module which was in the range of 200300 kWh/m 3 . The specific thermal energy consumption calculated for this system is less than that  for  conventional solar stills (640 kWh/m 3 ) [8]. Thus, MD is better energetically than solar stills.
texts.text: One of the most important characteristics of a thermal seawater-desalination plant is how much heat  it  uses  to  produce  a  specific  quantity  of distillate. The energy used for desalination in the existing system is totally supplied by the solar thermal collectors, the energy for auxiliary equipment as pumps and magnetic valves is supplied by  the  PV  panel.  The  specific  thermal  energy consumption  for  this  system  is  defined  as  the ratio of energy supplied by the solar collector to the  volume  of  distillate  produced.  The  heat recovery function is important to achieve a low specific  energy  consumption  of  the  membrane module which was in the range of 200300 kWh/m 3 . The specific thermal energy consumption calculated for this system is less than that  for  conventional solar stills (640 kWh/m 3 ) [8]. Thus, MD is better energetically than solar stills.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It  is  important  to  mention  that  the  distillate quality was almost the same as, if not better than, deionized water. The high water quality produced was almost independent of the salinity of the feed water. Similar results were reported by Banat et al. [4] who found that distillate quality was not affected by salt concentration in the feed stream.
texts.text: It  is  important  to  mention  that  the  distillate quality was almost the same as, if not better than, deionized water. The high water quality produced was almost independent of the salinity of the feed water. Similar results were reported by Banat et al. [4] who found that distillate quality was not affected by salt concentration in the feed stream.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Classical small-scale solar distillers have been used in remote sunny locations for more than a century but were with low thermal efficiency. To overcome such drawbacks, a PV and thermally driven small-scale, stand-alone compact desalination  system  with  internal  heat  recovery  was developed, installed, and tested in Irbid-Jordan. The system is autonomous and has been in operation  since  August  2005.  Whenever  sufficient sunlight  is  present  to  provide  hot  water  and electricity for pumping from the solar collectors and PV panels the unit starts to produce distillate water. The experiments carried out so far show very promising results. The production rate of the unit reached 120 L/d with a distillate quality of less  than  5  µS/cm.  The  distillate  flux  reached 19 L/d/m 2 ap. area with specific energy consumption in the range of 200-300 kWh/m 3 . The solar collector efficiency was about 45%.
texts.text: Classical small-scale solar distillers have been used in remote sunny locations for more than a century but were with low thermal efficiency. To overcome such drawbacks, a PV and thermally driven small-scale, stand-alone compact desalination  system  with  internal  heat  recovery  was developed, installed, and tested in Irbid-Jordan. The system is autonomous and has been in operation  since  August  2005.  Whenever  sufficient sunlight  is  present  to  provide  hot  water  and electricity for pumping from the solar collectors and PV panels the unit starts to produce distillate water. The experiments carried out so far show very promising results. The production rate of the unit reached 120 L/d with a distillate quality of less  than  5  µS/cm.  The  distillate  flux  reached 19 L/d/m 2 ap. area with specific energy consumption in the range of 200-300 kWh/m 3 . The solar collector efficiency was about 45%.
